title: India, Japan eye direct yen-rupee trade settlement to cut reliance on US dollar: Report
content:
• India and Japan are proposing a direct yen-rupee trade settlement framework to reduce reliance on the US dollar, which would allow residents to open accounts with Indian banks for direct cross-border payments.
• Japan announced a target of investing 10 trillion yen (approx ₹60,000 crore) in India over the coming decade, following Prime Minister Modi's visit to Tokyo in August 2025.
• Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi will visit New Delhi from July 1 to July 3 for the 16th India-Japan Annual Summit.

title: VB-G RAM G Act to come into force on July 1: Check revised state-wise rates under rural employment guarantee scheme
content:
• The Viksit Bharat Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin) Act, 2025 (VB-G RAM G Act, 2025) will come into force on July 1, replacing MGNREGA and guaranteeing 125 days of employment annually per eligible rural household.
• Under the VB-G RAM G Act, the revised national average wage has increased from ₹298.8 per day (under MGNREGA) to ₹327.4 per day, an average increase of ₹28.6 per day, effective July 1.
• A new interim base wage rate of ₹300 per day has been introduced under the VB-G RAM G Act, and an interim allocation of ₹95,692.31 crore has been made to states and Union Territories for its implementation.

title: US-Iran War News Live: Iran won't enter further negotiations until key provisions of MoU are implemented says Ghalibaf
content:
• Iran's Chief negotiator, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, revealed that Iran has exported over 40 million barrels of oil since the US blockade was lifted, after being unable to export any oil for the previous 50-60 days.
• Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi stated that Iran will pursue legal action against the US for the torpedo attack on the Iranian frigate IRIS Dena, which was sunk on March 4, killing 104 sailors.
• Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the former Supreme Leader of Iran, was killed by US-Israeli bombing in Tehran on February 28, with representatives from over 90 countries expressing readiness to attend his funeral.

title: World News Today Live Updates on July 1, 2026 : Israeli defence minister says Trump prevented Israel from eliminating Hezbollah in Lebanon: Report
content:
• World News on July 1, 2026, reported that the Israeli defence minister stated Donald Trump prevented Israel from eliminating Hezbollah in Lebanon.

title: Israeli defence minister says Trump prevented Israel from eliminating Hezbollah in Lebanon: Report
content:
• Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz claimed that US President Donald Trump prevented Israel from fully eliminating Hezbollah in Lebanon during its military campaign.
• Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu resisted US President Trump's demands to scale back military operations in Lebanon through four phone calls.
• Last week, Israel and Lebanon agreed that Israeli forces could remain in southern Lebanon until Hezbollah is defeated.

title: Armie Hammer comeback film 'Citizen Vigilante' lands global deal following Elon Musk promotion
content:
• The film 'Citizen Vigilante' generated approximately $600,000 in revenue through digital platforms since its North American release, against an estimated production budget of around $2 million.
• Quiver Distribution acquired worldwide rights to 'Citizen Vigilante' (excluding the United Kingdom, German-speaking territories, South Korea, and Taiwan), having already released the film in North America on June 19.
• Elon Musk promoted 'Citizen Vigilante' by sharing it in its entirety on his social media platform X, allowing free downloads for a 48-hour period.

title: US Supreme Court to decide whether Americans have a right to own AR-15-style assault rifles
content:
• The US Supreme Court has agreed to hear challenges to assault weapon bans in Connecticut (enacted after the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting) and Cook County, Illinois (adopted in 1993).
• A ruling against these bans by the Supreme Court could lead to similar laws being struck down in approximately a dozen states and several major US cities.
• This case is significant as it follows the Supreme Court's landmark 2022 decision which significantly expanded Second Amendment protections for firearm ownership.

title: Trump calls Supreme Court birthright citizenship ruling ‘too bad,’ urges Congress to end citizenship by law
content:
• The US Supreme Court, on Tuesday (June 30), upheld birthright citizenship as guaranteed by the 14th Amendment, rejecting President Donald Trump's executive order aimed at limiting it.
• The 14th Amendment, ratified in 1868, states that "All persons born or naturalised in the United States, and subject to the jurisdiction thereof, are citizens of the United States."
• The Supreme Court's ruling was a 5-4 majority decision, with Chief Justice John Roberts writing the opinion, and would have impacted nearly 250,000 babies born in the United States each year had the executive order been upheld.

title: US Supreme Court blocks Trump's Birthright Citizenship order: What it means for Indians
content:
• The US Supreme Court on Tuesday, June 30, struck down President Trump's executive order that intended to end birthright citizenship.
• Birthright citizenship, a 160-year practice granting citizenship to anyone born on American soil, is enshrined within the US Constitution through the 14th Amendment.
• Chief Justice John Roberts authored the majority opinion, preserving the understanding that anyone born on US soil is automatically entitled to citizenship.

title: US Supreme Court strikes down federal limits on political party spending coordinated with candidates
content:
• On Tuesday (June 30), the US Supreme Court struck down federal limits on political party spending coordinated with candidates for Congress and the presidency.
• The decision removes restrictions on coordinated party spending that had been in place for over 50 years, significantly reshaping how political parties fund federal election campaigns.
• Prior to the ruling, for the 2024 election cycle, Senate races had spending limits ranging from $127,200 to nearly $4 million, and House races from $63,600 to $127,200. The case was brought by Republican Party committees, including JD Vance.
